summary: Gruber examines the near-fiasco where West Point's PR team falsely told ProPublica that Pete Hegseth had never applied to West Point, when he had in fact been accepted in 1999. He argues ProPublica should have still published a story — not about Hegseth lying, but about West Point PR's serious error. Gruber contends this was likely an honest mistake rather than deliberate sabotage, but notes the lack of accountability inherent in attributing statements to institutions rather than named individuals. He connects this to The Verge's 2021 policy of making all communications on-the-record by default, reconsidering his earlier skepticism of that stance. His core argument is that requiring spokespeople to put their names on statements would reduce both mistakes and lies, because personal accountability changes behavior.
key_points: ProPublica acted correctly by not publishing a false story, but the incident itself — West Point PR twice falsely denying Hegseth's application — was still a story worth reporting.
Attributing statements to institutions rather than named individuals creates a dangerous accountability gap that enables both careless mistakes and potential dishonesty.
The Verge's policy of requiring on-the-record attribution for all official communications is vindicated by this incident — personal stakes make people more careful.
The West Point error was almost certainly a mistake rather than deliberate sabotage, given that West Point falls under the Defense Department that Hegseth would lead.
Putting your name on your work — whether journalism, PR statements, or anything else — is a sign of responsibility and a signifier of quality.
money_quotes: When a statement is attributed to 'a spokesperson' from a company or institution, the world doesn't know who that spokesperson is. Only the reporter or writer, and perhaps their editors. There is an explicit lack of accountability attributing statements to an institution rather than to specific people.
We even have different pronouns — it's institutions that do things, but only people who do things. Who is the question.
'The Yankees' didn't drop a routine fly ball that cost them the World Series. Aaron Judge did — and he has taken full responsibility and accountability for it.

Putting one's name on a statement heightens the personal stakes. This is why it's more than vanity to put your name on your work, whatever your work is — it shows you take responsibility for its validity.

I can't help but think they'd have been less likely to make the mistake — more likely to have double-checked whatever records West Point keeps — if their own names were on the line, not just 'West Point'.
Even if I'm wrong and it was a lie intended to sabotage Hegseth's nomination, it's almost impossible to imagine anyone committing that lie with their name attached to it.
key_insight: The practice of attributing statements to unnamed institutional spokespeople rather than named individuals creates an accountability vacuum that makes both honest mistakes and deliberate dishonesty more likely.
